label: Plaza de las Tres Culturas
sub: Primary massacre site — October 2, 1968. The plaza (Three Cultures Square) sits at the center of the Tlatelolco housing complex and is flanked by pre-Columbian ruins, the colonial Santiago Tlatelolco church, and modern housing towers. Thousands were gathered here for a peaceful CNH rally when the Olympia Battalion and army opened fire.

label: Chihuahua Building
sub: Residential tower at Tlatelolco from whose upper floors Olympia Battalion snipers fired into both the crowd and army units below on October 2. CNH student leaders were also speaking from a balcony of this building when they were arrested mid-speech by Olympia Battalion agents.

label: Ciudad Universitaria (UNAM)
sub: Main campus of the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM). The epicenter of the 1968 student movement — site of the July 26 Granaderos assault that sparked the movement, the August 30 army occupation, and dozens of rallies. CNH coordinated much of its activity from here. Now houses the Museo Universitario Arte Contemporáneo (MUAC).

label: IPN Casco de Santo Tomás
sub: Main campus of the Instituto Politécnico Nacional (IPN). Students here were involved in the original July 22 brawl with Granaderos. The army occupied this campus on September 23, 1968, violating institutional autonomy. IPN students were crucial members of the CNH.

label: Zócalo (Plaza de la Constitución)
sub: Mexico City's central plaza and symbolic heart of Mexican political life. Site of the massive August 27 student march (50,000–100,000 attendees) and subsequent confrontations when the army cleared overnight occupiers. Annual October 2 commemorative marches converge here before proceeding to Tlatelolco.

label: Lecumberri Prison (Palacio Negro)
sub: Historic penitentiary ('Black Palace of Lecumberri') where hundreds of student activists and CNH leaders were held following the massacre, many for months or years without trial under 'social dissolution' laws. CNH leaders including Félix Hernández Gamundi and Pablo Gómez were imprisoned here. The building was converted into the Archivo General de la Nación (AGN) in 1980 — the archive now holds the DFS secret police files used in investigations.

label: Campo Militar No. 1
sub: Military Camp No. 1, in the western part of Mexico City. Key detention site for disappeared persons from the 1968 massacre and subsequent 'dirty war.' Bodies from Tlatelolco are alleged to have been transported here overnight. During the broader dirty war (1968–1982), hundreds of political prisoners were held, tortured, and disappeared here. The army has consistently refused to open its archives related to this facility.

label: Los Pinos (Presidential Residence)
sub: Presidential residence and seat of executive power at the time of the massacre. From here, President Gustavo Díaz Ordaz coordinated the government's response to the student movement. Interior Secretary Echeverría maintained close communication with Los Pinos throughout the events of 1968. The facility was converted into a public cultural center under AMLO in 2018.

label: Secretaría de Gobernación (Interior Ministry)
sub: The Interior Ministry, led by Luis Echeverría Álvarez in 1968. This ministry coordinated the DFS (secret police), oversaw intelligence operations against the student movement, managed the press blackout following the massacre, and orchestrated the Olympia Battalion operation. Echeverría used this ministry as his base for directing the repression.

label: Palacio Nacional
sub: Mexico's National Palace, seat of the executive branch where the president's offices are located. Díaz Ordaz issued his September 1, 1968 warning to students from the adjacent assembly hall. Annual October 2 commemorations march past this building.

label: Archivo General de la Nación (AGN)
sub: The National Archive of Mexico, located in the former Lecumberri Prison since 1980. The AGN holds the Galería 1 collection — declassified DFS secret police files on the 1968 student movement and massacre. These files, released beginning in 2002 under Fox's government, have been central to all serious investigations. Researchers from Mexico and abroad use these archives to document the coverup.

label: Centro Cultural Universitario Tlatelolco
sub: UNAM-operated cultural center adjacent to Plaza de las Tres Culturas that houses the '68 permanent exhibition — one of Mexico's most important human rights memorial sites. The exhibition opened October 2, 2007 (39th anniversary) and contains thousands of photographs, documents, testimonies, and audiovisual materials documenting the student movement and massacre. Free admission.

label: Vocacional 5 (IPN)
sub: The IPN's Vocational High School No. 5 near the Centro Médico. Site of the July 22, 1968 brawl between students and Granaderos riot police that sparked the entire 1968 student movement. The police attack on students celebrating a football victory was the proximate cause of the movement.

label: Escuela Nacional Preparatoria (San Ildefonso)
sub: UNAM's historic preparatory school in Centro Histórico, one of several student institutions involved in the early phases of the 1968 movement. Granaderos used a bazooka to blow open the colonial-era doors on July 29, 1968 — an act of desecration that shocked Mexican society and intensified support for the student movement.

label: Monument to the Revolution
sub: Iconic monument and gathering point for political demonstrations. Starting point for numerous student marches in 1968 and the annual October 2 commemorative marches. The area around the monument was frequently used by student groups for rallying.

label: DFS Headquarters (Former)
sub: Headquarters of the Dirección Federal de Seguridad (DFS) — Mexico's feared secret police, which reported to the Interior Ministry. The DFS coordinated surveillance and infiltration of the student movement in 1968 and maintained close liaison with the CIA. DFS files now housed at AGN are the primary primary source for investigating the massacre and broader dirty war.
